question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any maintenance programme that exists for the removal of buddleia and other large plants. growing from the West Link wall in Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline any assessment his Department has made of the reported figures from HM Treasury, in relation to the open-book exercise, that health spending per capita in Northern Ireland is 152 per cent of that in England.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been made of the damage being caused to the West Link wall from buddleia and other large plants growing from it, further to the collapse of part of the outer wall at Brown Square.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of ministerial visits she undertook, during the 2025-26 financial year.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's policy on the long-term parking of caravans on the carriageway of residential streets, particularly where this is causing a safety hazard for drivers.
answer_text: My Department recognises the concern that long-term parking of caravans on residential streets can reduce visibility, create obstruction and present a risk to road users, particularly at junctions, bends and on narrow carriageways.There is no blanket prohibition on parking a caravan on a public road; however, any vehicle parked on the public road must comply with enforceable parking restrictions in place at that location (for example, restrictions introduced through traffic regulation orders). Caravans attached to vehicles and/or displaying a Vehicle Registration Mark (VRM) are classed as motor vehicles and subject to any parking restrictions that may be in force. Where such restrictions apply, my Department can take enforcement action. Article 91 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence, without reasonable excuse, to encamp on a road (including with a caravan).  Caravans parked between sunset and sunrise must comply with the Road Vehicles Lighting Regulations (NI) 2000 (Regulation 27), and must not cause unnecessary obstruction (Regulation 119 of the Motor Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations (NI) 1999).Concerns that the position of a caravan is causing a danger, or is obstructing the road, should be reported to the PSNI who have the appropriate powers to address dangerous or obstructive parking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment that has been made on whether the current legislation is adequate to respond to the danger posed by the use of electric scramblers and electric scooters in public spaces.
answer_text: The regulation of scramblers and e-scooters, including the assessment of current legislation and enforcement powers, is primarily a matter for the Department for Infrastructure.  My Department is happy to work with the Department for Infrastructure to confirm that if any new offences and penalties are proposed, they are proportionate and consistent with existing law.Police constables have existing powers to seize vehicles (including scramblers and e-scooters) used in a manner causing alarm, distress or annoyance. These powers are included at Articles 65 and 66 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008.My Department is aware of proposals within the Crime and Policing Bill, which I understand is now in its final Parliamentary stages, to amend vehicle seizure powers in England and Wales, as well as the UK Government’s recent consultation proposals to increase statutory charges, and reduce the disposal timeframes, for seized vehicles.My Department will consider the need to make equivalent changes to the vehicle seizure regime in Northern Ireland, in consultation with the Police Service for Northern Ireland and the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on measures to provide assistance to people during the current increase in energy costs.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any assessment that has been made of the environmental impact of long-term derelict properties, particularly in urban areas.
answer_text: A 2018 Ulster University study commissioned by my department sought to quantify the scale of the problem of dilapidated buildings here both in terms of property numbers and the costs of remediation. 		This was followed by a 2019 study for the Scottish Land Commission on the impact of vacant and derelict land on communities overall. This study highlighted long-term negative impacts of dereliction including: anti-social behaviour; population decline; poor health outcomes; degraded local ecosystems; increased pollution risks; reduced investment; and depressed regeneration activity.	Other studies have flagged increased fire risk and reduced footfall as well as a correlation with reduced wellbeing, lower community cohesion and reduced neighbourhood satisfaction.	The Dilapidation Bill which I am currently progressing has an important role to play with regard to public health, avoidance of danger, visual amenity, tourism, regeneration and the reduction of anti-social behaviour by giving district councils modern and robust powers to deal with the scourge of dilapidation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the replacement of Finlock guttering in the Ballysillan area, North Belfast.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of additional capital funding her Department has received for water infrastructure, in each year of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate, including a breakdown of how that funding has been allocated by geographic area.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. The table below outlines the additional capital funding that Department has provided NI Water in the period 2022-2026, in the context of the full capital DEL budget allocation. The budget position for 2026-2027 is yet to be determined.YEAROPENING POSITION£kADDITIONAL CAPITAL £kOTHER FUNDING £kTOTALCAPITAL £k2021-22215,0006,210815222,0252022-23230,65951,3418,265290,2652023-24321,2009,9766,110337,2862024-25323,66719,5005,615348,7822025-26350,00018,5331,150369,683This additional funding was invested in wastewater infrastructure to help unlock housing capacity across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Royal Mail deliveries have become less frequent, to detail any representations he has made to Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure that they are not relying solely on the postal service for the communication of hospital and medical appointments.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working with the Health and Social Care Trusts to continually improve communication methods with all patients, ensuring that they have the best possible experience and timely access to care.Most outpatient services, within Health and Social Care Trusts provide a partial booking process, whereby a patient is asked to contact the Trust by phone and has an opportunity to agree and confirm the date and time of their appointment. This approach gives patients greater control and awareness of their scheduled appointments, helping to reduce reliance on postal notifications and minimise missed appointments caused by potential postal delivery delays.To help improve patient communication, my Department has provided recurrent funding to Trusts to support the implementation of a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In conjunction with sending appointment letters to patients, Trusts frequently use text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments across most services. This approach aims to provide a timely and convenient method of communication, especially when postal services may be delayed, helping to reduce the number of ‘Did Not Attend’ (DNAs) by prompting attendance and giving patients the opportunity to cancel or reschedule their appointments, ensuring valuable appointment slots can be offered to other patients.Going forward, with Encompass now fully operational across Northern Ireland, all patients and service users will be able to securely access a subset of their Health and Social Care record via the My Care application, which will include information such as appointment letters, medication details, and test results. They can also view appointments with the aim of reducing DNAs. Trusts are encouraging patients and service users to sign up for the My Care application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of the new Belfast Maternity Hospital, originally proposed as £57 million.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has a timescale for when the new Belfast Maternity Hospital will be ready for public use.

To ask the Minister of Justice, following legislative changes made elsewhere in the United Kingdom, whether she has any plans to review the use of Non-Disclosure Agreements and Non-Disparagement Agreements in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any steps being taken to increase car parking for people attending events at Parliament Buildings on sitting days, particularly for people with disabilities.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission fully acknowledges the difficulties experienced with parking, particularly on sitting days. Although the Assembly Commission will continue to look at ways in which those difficulties might be eased, there are no immediate plans to increase the number of spaces available, as doing so is not considered to be economically viable, owing to the topography and the limited space in general around the building.The Assembly Commission currently has the use of four car parks in the vicinity of Parliament Buildings.  They are the two upper car parks, located on either side of the main Building, with a third, which is referred to as the lower east, or visitors', car park, situated a short distance away from the main Building, near the search facility.  Finally, an overspill car park near the lower east car park is also readily accessible.  Following a review, the number of accessible spaces in the upper east car park was increased from four to six during the summer of 2025 and a similar increase is planned for the upper west car park following the completion of the roof project. Visitors can also avail of drop-off and pick up in proximity to one of the building entrances if necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any additional powers or legislation the PSNI has proposed in relation to her intention to repeal relevant provisions in the Vagrancy Act 1824.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department has provided for the new Ligoniel Cultural Hub, in North Belfast.
answer_text: My department has contributed a total of £74,000 to Belfast City Council for the development of a new Cultural Hub and Community Facility on the derelict site at Upper Crumlin Road.  This is part of an overall funding package of £450,000.00.  The contractor has been appointed, and work is expected to start in February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the status of negotiations with HM Treasury in relation to the retention of funds from tackling benefit fraud and error.
answer_text: There is a longstanding commitment within the Fresh Start Agreement, that the Executive could bid for a share of savings from actions carried out to reduce benefit fraud and error, where these savings could be verified by the Office for Budget Responsibility. On the basis of this commitment, my department provided the business case, prepared by the Department for Communities, to the Treasury, making the case for such savings. Treasury indicated that there are some issues in relation to the business case that it wishes to explore in more detail. A terms of reference in relation to this further work has now been agreed with Treasury and my officials are liaising with DfC officials on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the replacement of Finlock guttering on Housing Executive and former Housing Executive homes in Ballysillan, North Belfast.
answer_text: Contractors appointed by the Housing Executive have completed detailed surveys at each affected property. These surveys have confirmed that the cost to remove and replace the Finlock guttering is higher than originally predicted. The cost per property is now above £5,000. The work to the properties is challenging because the Finlock gutters are, in several instances, shared between NIHE and private properties, and the guttering along the entire frontage must be remediated.My officials met with the Housing Executive on Monday 26 January to discuss and explore what options are available in relation to the additional costs. The Department will continue to work with the Housing Executive to achieve a resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to increase the availability of NHS dentistry in North Belfast.
answer_text: I acknowledge that access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present, however it is important to highlight that General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of HS care they provide. The Department plays no role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.In May 2025 I announced a £7m investment in General Dental Services for 2025/26. This investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and the continuation of a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals; alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027. As part of that work, consideration will be given to other actions and initiatives which could improve access to HS dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether hospital beds are reserved for inpatient cancer treatment in order to prevent the cancellation of critical appointments and waste of resources.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any criteria her Department applies to approve grit boxes in public streets and spaces.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria-based system which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any criteria used by her Department to determine which roads are part of the gritting network in icy weather.
answer_text: The roads selected for salting and the schedule for gritted routes on the network have been generated through the application of a specifically designed set of criteria to determine if a section of public road should be included.This Primary Salting Network is made up of higher-trafficked main through-routes, such as Motorways and Trunk Roads, main routes carrying more than 1,500 vehicles per day, and busy routes carrying 1,000 -1,500 vehicles per day with special difficulties such as railway crossings, and excessive gradients. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted.Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion.It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.The full Winter Service Policy is available to view on my Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a further update on the roll-out of multi-disciplinary teams in GP practices in North Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027, for his assessment of whether livestock numbers should be reduced.
answer_text: Tackling climate change is one of my key priorities and critically important for the agriculture sector, which is both affected by the consequences of extreme weather events, whilst also having a positive and constructive role to play in reducing our greenhouse gas emissions.I am committed to delivering on the requirements of Northern Ireland’s Climate Change Act. Delivering net zero will bring significant benefits for the people of Northern Ireland, our economy and our environment. The Climate Change Committee (CCC) has proposed a transition to low carbon farming practices within the agriculture sector and significant reductions in livestock numbers in its advice to Northern Ireland to reach net zero by 2050.  My Department, while promoting low carbon practices, is following a different pathway in relation to reductions in livestock numbers. As outlined in the draft Climate Action Plan, emissions from the agriculture sector will be reduced through the schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP). These aim to deliver emissions reductions through improvements to livestock productivity on farms, resulting in targeted livestock reductions (by removal of non-productive and older cattle), while allowing the agriculture sector to maintain output, avoid carbon leakage, and contribute to the growing global demand for livestock food products projected by the Food and Agriculture Organisation, (FAO).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the criteria being applied for one-year Temporary Exemption Certificates.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any measures local councils should take to respond to unlawful damage to council property.
answer_text: Any measures to be taken in response to unlawful damage to council property are a matter for the relevant council and, if a crime has been committed, the Police Service of Northern Ireland.My department has no remit regarding damage to council property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the contribution of the defence industry in driving forward innovation within the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: According to the ADS Group’s 2024 Northern Ireland Facts & Figures bulletin, up to 1,000 people are directly employed in the defence industry across the North, contributing £100m in revenues annually.The North’s advanced manufacturing sector benefits from a skilled workforce and a strong engineering heritage, and the region is also recognised as a leader in innovation in areas such as cyber, data analytics, autonomy, and secure communications.The success of businesses operating here reflects the quality of our industrial base and its ability to meet complex technical demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's support for community development in Glencairn, North Belfast.
answer_text: I am delighted to reaffirm my support for the community in Glencairn and was privileged to personally meet a great range of children, local residents, and community leaders at the recent launch of the Glencairn Place Shaping Plan.The Place Shaping plan, developed by the Housing Executive, sets out a clear and practical approach for working together to improve the area.  It reflects what the local community wants and provides a strategy to tackle long-standing problems.I am supporting the development of the Glencairn Community Hub, in collaboration with Belfast City Council, with an investment of £430k during 2025/26.  This is a key capital development in the area and will provide a modern community-based facility for the delivery of local community activities.Glencairn is part of the Greater Shankill Neighbourhood Renewal Area, and Neighbourhood Renewal grant funding of £156k for Greater Shankill Partnership Board enables it to continue to support community development and community organisations in Glencairn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the supply of veterinary medicines from Great Britain after the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the progress made in securing funding towards the PSNI workforce recovery costs in 2025-26, further to the Executive agreeing in the June monitoring round that the Department for Justice would be given first call up on £7 million for this purpose, in future monitoring rounds.
answer_text: The Executive agreed in June Monitoring that my Department would be given first call up to £7 million for the PSNI workforce recovery costs. This position has been reaffirmed at subsequent Executive meetings. My Department is therefore planning on the basis that formal confirmation of this funding allocation will be received in the upcoming December Monitoring Round, which was commissioned by the Department of Finance on 22 October 2025. This funding, alongside the Finance Minster’s recommendation that the rest of the Recovery Plan is fully funded in the pending three-year Executive budget, should ensure that the PSNI gets the additional officer and staff numbers it needs in full and on time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the public facility that will exist for pick-up and drop-off by car drivers at Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) where will this be located.
answer_text: Once the works in Phase 2 at Belfast Grand Central Station are complete there will be dedicated drop-off and pick-up provisions for private vehicles together with a taxi pick-up/drop-off point on Durham Street as well as at the car park to the rear of the station. These improvements are all aimed at making the station more accessible and user-friendly for everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development plan for Seaview Primary School, North Belfast.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when Durham Street, Belfast is expected to reopen for traffic.
answer_text: I welcome that Translink have been working closely with its contractor to accelerate the work programme with the aim of reopening Durham Street by the end of November 2025.While the reopening of Durham Street will help ease traffic build-up in that part of the Belfast, we should still expect additional traffic on our roads in the run up to Christmas.  With this in mind, I have agreed to bring forward the road works embargo to 10 November 2025 within the Belfast City Council area.We can all play a part by thinking about when to travel and how. Avoid peak times if you can and think about leaving the car at home and use public transport and park and ride facilities, if they are available to you.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the extreme traffic congestion on Royal Avenue, Belfast, to outline the measures her Department is taking to prevent private buses parking for a prolonged period in the bus lane, outside the Europa Hotel.
answer_text: The Europa Hotel is on Great Victoria Street in Belfast and my Department deploys Traffic Attendants daily to enforce illegal parking including parking on bus lane restrictions at this location. The Traffic Attendant will often speak with drivers and request that they move off the restriction in the first instance thereby avoiding the immediate issuing of a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN).  However, when the driver is not present or refuses to move the vehicle, a PCN will be issued.  Our parking enforcement approach is flexible and, resource permitting, can be adjusted to seek to address identified parking issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the commencement of the Shankill Gateway Public Realm Scheme in Belfast.
answer_text: The contractor for the Shankill Gateway Public Realm scheme has now been appointed.  Minor preparatory works were carried out in June 2025 to ensure the planning permission remained valid.  While some limited construction activity may take place towards the end of 2025, the main works are scheduled to commence in January 2026 and are expected to take approximately 12 months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current protocols for Social Services communicating collaboratively with school teachers and principals when (i) updating them when circumstances change; (ii) a serious incident has occurred in a child's life; and (iii) when Social Services have closed a case.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he changed, or in any way affected, the merit-ranked order of applications from Performance Clubs for stadia funding from The Northern Ireland Football Fund after they were scored by officials and checked by an independent moderator.
answer_text: I was not involved in scoring applications or in determining their merit order. My role in this process was to approve the overarching policy approach and to decide how many projects would be taken forward to the next stage. I did not see any of the applications and I did not influence or seek to influence the merit order of any of the tiers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Dilapidation Bill (NIA Bill 21/22-27) will strengthen statutory powers to deal with long-term derelict, privately-owned houses in residential areas.
answer_text: The existing legislation available to councils, much of which dates from the mid-19th century and/or applies to specific geographical areas, is limited in its scope and effectiveness and subject to differences in interpretation between councils, making some very reluctant to use it at all. It is less potent than the legislation in force in the rest of the UK and therefore no longer fit for purpose and is seen as an obstacle to the revitalisation of towns and cities across Northern Ireland.		The Dilapidation Bill will consolidate, amend and enhance the powers currently available to councils. By giving councils new robust and effective cost recovery powers it will be more likely that they will use their powers under the Bill, now that the likelihood of a council being disadvantaged financially has been reduced. 		These powers will be available to councils in cases of derelict, privately-owned houses in residential areas if a council is minded to take the necessary enforcement action.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the request for a new pedestrian crossing on Twaddell Avenue, Belfast, in the vicinity of Donaldson Crescent.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many areas that would benefit from investment relating to pedestrian crossings, due to limited budget resources, work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.An assessment was undertaken on 4th October 2023 and regrettably this location did not meet the minimum requirement for a pedestrian crossing.Details of the schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's support for the Glencairn community in North Belfast.
answer_text: I am hugely supportive of the community sector in Greater Shankill, and I am delighted to support the development of the Glencairn Community Hub in partnership with Belfast City Council with an investment of £430k during 2025/26.From February 2024 to 31 March 2026, £2.75m funding to Voluntary and Community organisations in a range of funding programmes in the Belfast North of which Glencairn community is a part.Ulster Supported Employment Limited North Belfast delivers a range of employability and skills provision to disabled people and people with health conditions from the surrounding catchment area.During 2024/25 Sport NI have provided a total of £5,000 in Lottery funding to Rathcoole Amateur Boxing Club in North Belfast and during 2025/26, Sport NI have provided £3,382 to Woodvale Youth Academy and £5,000 to Woodvale Cricket Club.The Housing Executive have completed two planned maintenance schemes in North Belfast totalling approximately £955,000. Currently they are on site as part of the external cyclical maintenance scheme in Glencairn totalling approximately £1.37million.My Department has provided placemaking support to co-create the Greater Shankill Living High Streets Framework, using the ‘MAG Living High Streets Craft Kit’ process. I have provided funding to Labour Market Partnerships of £1,204,718 in 2024/25 and £1,097,940 in 2025/26 to Belfast City Council to deliver employability provision and help tackle economic inactivity in Belfast including the Glencairn community in North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any plans for a new pedestrian crossing on the Crumlin Road, Belfast, in the vicinity of Cambrai Street and Flax Street.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the percentage of days that Northern Ireland Civil Service staff have worked from their physical workplace in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his departmental funding allocated to the Glencairn Community Hub in North Belfast, which is also part-funded by Belfast City Council.
answer_text: As part of my departmental budgetary announcement on 2nd June 2025, I was pleased to support the development of the Glencairn Community Hub in partnership with Belfast City Council (BCC).  I understand that Department officials have recently been in discussion with Belfast City Council regarding a potential shortfall in the funding originally allocated to the project.Belfast City Council (BCC) is the project lead and has confirmed the required contribution from DfC for the project has risen to £430k.  Officials are currently working with BCC on project details and intend to issue a letter of offer to BCC for £430k on completion of internal technical and legal activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the dates on which the Urban Development Grant has been open for applications during the last five years; and (ii) the criteria and conditions that existed on each occasion.
answer_text: There has been no open call for Urban Development Grant applications issued by my department within the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding her Department has provided to NI Water, in each of the last 10 years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to reducing the height of the Ardoyne roundabout, North Belfast, in the interest of traffic safety.
answer_text: My officials consider the roundabout arrangement to be suitable and to meet the traffic demands in the area.  In addition, a review of the recorded road traffic collision history did not indicate a particular safety concern.As such there are no plans at present to make any changes or adjustments to the roundabout.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when was the last assessment of the Ardoyne roundabout, North Belfast, undertaken.
answer_text: On an annual basis, my Department undertakes a review of the road network with regard to collisions involving personal injury. This is completed to identify common patterns of collisions that can be treated by engineering methods and bring forward associated improvement schemes.Officials consider the Ardoyne roundabout arrangement to be suitable and to meet traffic demands in the area.  In addition, a review of the recorded road traffic collision history did not indicate a particular safety concern.As such there are no plans at present to make any changes or adjustments to the roundabout.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Intermediate Rent scheme to increase supply of affordable private rented housing.

To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the length of time it takes for additional police officers, requested under mutual aid arrangements from elsewhere in the United Kingdom, to provide support on the ground.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 15 - AQO 2227/22-27		  ANSWER The deployment of additional officers from other regions of the United Kingdom, requested under mutual aid arrangements, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.   I would recommend the question be better directed to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his discussions with HM Treasury on the opportunity to bid for a share of fraud and error savings in social security benefits.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the schedule for spraying weeds on streets in the North Belfast constituency in 2025; and (ii) when the first spray is scheduled to (a) begin; and (b) end.
answer_text: My Department undertakes routine weed control on the adopted road network, including footways, throughout the North.  Weed control is undertaken annually and normally results in weeds being sprayed once per year, with further treatments carried out, as required, following an assessment of the effectiveness of the initial action.My officials are currently in discussions with the environmental maintenance contractor regarding arrangements for commencement of spraying operations in the North Belfast constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to the October Monitoring Allocation of £19.5 million to NI Water to focus on unlocking capacity for 2,300 new properties, to detail which electoral wards and areas this funding has been allocated to.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has provided the table below to advise of those projects which have been allocated with the £19.5m capital funding. NI Water’s systems capture capital projects by Council area, not electoral ward, so the Council area of each project has been provided in the table. You should also note that, while the projects commenced in 2024-25, for some projects the full investment will be spread across the remaining years of PC21 (2021-28): Project Investment (£m) * Council Area Upper Falls WwTW Stormwater Storage 7.0 Belfast City Council Faughan Crescent Pumping Station 2.1 Derry City & Strabane District Council Park Manor Pumping Station 0.5 Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council Mill Road CSO 7.4 Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council Templepatrick phase on Antrim Drainage project 0.5 Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council Cabragh WwTW 2.8 Mid Ulster District Council The Investment value provides the full cost of each project to which the additional allocation has been applied. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any responsibility her Department has for the access road to Ligoniel Primary School from Ligoniel Road, North Belfast; and (ii) whether her Department will carry out any maintenance repairs in the interest of pupil and parent safety, given the deteriorating condition of this access road.
answer_text: My officials have advised that the access road to Ligoniel Primary School from Ligoniel Road is not adopted or maintained by my Department. As such my Department has no maintenance responsibility for this access road.You will be aware that my department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years, and as such our limited resources have been directed to the maintenance of the adopted network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any incidents of solar geoengineering spraying have taken place in the skies above Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am not aware of any incidents of solar geoengineering spraying in the skies above Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the priority allocated to a new pedestrian crossing on the Crumlin Road, Belfast, in the vicinity of Crimea Street and Flax Street, providing safer access to Hillview Retail Park.
answer_text: Details of the schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any benefits arising from recent engagements in Washington DC in advance of St. Patrick's Day.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline when the last review of security arrangements for Parliament Buildings took place.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any benefits arising from his recent engagements in Washington DC in advance of St. Patrick's Day.

To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the Enabling Access to Justice Programme, to provide an update on negotiations with the Criminal Bar Association and the working group regarding the level of remuneration.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of a suitable new centre for the Inclusion Health Service in Belfast.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether (i) preparatory works; and (ii) the future design of Carlisle Circus are included in her statement on the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 on 25 February 2025.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement on 25 February 2025, the initial phase of the work on the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 project will focus on delivering best value utilising the £35m from the Belfast Region City Deal and an additional commitment of £13m from my departmental budget for the delivery of the proposed Park and Ride at O’Neill Road.Due to the traffic management implications of the work associated with the Carlisle Circus junction, it is important to ensure that the delivery of this signalisation is planned to reduce the impact on the travelling public and to minimise congestion levels in and around the city centre.This work will therefore not form part of the initial phase of the project, but will be timed for completion in advance of full operation of the North/South Glider route.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any plans to attend events in Washington DC for St. Patrick's Day.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Clifton Gateway Public Realm scheme.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline which sectors of the economy have the greatest shortage of supply of skilled workers.
answer_text: Question 15 – AQO 1524/22-27The undersupply of skilled workers is the biggest challenge facing businesses right across the North and is an area that I am fully committed to addressing.The latest economic evidence shows that the local labour market continues to remain challenging, with an annual undersupply of over four thousand workers, with unemployment remaining historically low at 1.7% and economic inactivity is persistently high at 26.1%. This is a challenge faced by all sectors in our economy, including Transport and Storage, Construction and Manufacturing facing the most significant shortages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of policing hours that have been spent at hospital Emergency Departments in the (i) North Belfast; (ii) South Belfast; (iii) East Belfast; and (iv) West Belfast, area in the last six months.
answer_text: My Department does not collate or hold this information.This is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I am committed to respecting the operational responsibilities of both organisations, you may therefore wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department is taking to curb the recent interface clashes between youths at Carlisle Circus, Townsend Street, North Howard Street and Northumberland Street, Belfast.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 14 - AQO 1479/22-27		ANSWERI am aware of the recent escalation in ASB and disorder around the areas mentioned. My officials, alongside PSNI colleagues, have been monitoring the situation on an almost daily basis.The PSNI have informed us that they have ASB patrols in the area and are liaising with the Education Authority and Belfast City Council in order to brief youth workers and safer neighbourhood officers.Additionally, the District Policing and Community Safety Partnership in North Belfast have been working with funded groups to provide additional deployments to engage young people; focusing on Townsend St, North Howard St, Northumberland St, Henry Place and Denmark St.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline which sectors offer the greatest growth potential for the Northern Ireland economy in 2025.
answer_text: Question 9 – AQO 1385/22-27In June 2024, I launched Sectoral Action Plans for seven of the most innovative, productive and international focused sectors of the North’s economy. These sectors are Agri-Tech, Life and Health Sciences, Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering, Fintech and Financial Services, Software and Cyber, Screen Industries, and Low Carbon/net zero. The Social Enterprise sector also offers huge economic growth potential, therefore in December, I launched my Department’s three year Social Enterprise Action Plan.I recognise how the Tourism sector has the potential to contribute to our economic objectives and I therefore launched my Tourism Vision and Action Plan, on Wednesday 15 January, which sets out how we plan to grow our tourism sector over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on funding via the Urban Villages Initiative for the new Westland Community Centre in North Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department's funding via the Belfast Region City Deal for North Belfast.
answer_text: The £1 billion Belfast Region City Deal is made up of £350 million from the Executive and £350 million from the British Government plus Deal partner funding. The Deal covers six council areas, which includes the Belfast North constituency.Out of some 20 Belfast Region City Deal projects, three are within the Belfast North constituency:Studio Ulster on the North Foreshore has a funding allocation of £28.8 million and has been drawing down funding in anticipation of opening in early 2025.The Centre for Digital Healthcare Technology, which will be built across from the Ulster University Campus in York Street, has a funding allocation of £40.1million. Once the legally binding contract for funding has been signed, funding will begin to flow.Work has completed on the feasibility of extending the Belfast Rapid Transit to Glengormley and Carryduff through a £35 million project. The Department for Infrastructure is currently developing delivery options that could be implemented in line with these feasibility reports and delivery options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the resurfacing work at Townsend Street will cover all of the street up to Peter’s Hill on the Shankill Road, Belfast.
answer_text: A £120k carriageway resurfacing scheme was recently completed on Townsend Street, Belfast by the Department.The planned scheme was to include the resurfacing of the entire stretch of Townsend Street, from Divis Street to Peter’s Hill, however my officials were notified of development works within the original scheme plan which included excavations for utility connections and sewage provision.The scheme was subsequently shortened to take account of the development works and to minimise any potential damage to the newly resurfaced carriageway.  The scheme now extends from Divis street to Townsend Enterprise Park, just before the proposed development works.The remaining section of Townsend Street, from Townsend Enterprise Park to Peter’s Hill, estimated to cost in the region of £60,000, will be assessed once development works have been completed with a view to inclusion in a resurfacing programme for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what progress is being made, in association with Lidl, to ensure that the security gate between Hillview Retail Park and Flax Street, Belfast, can be closed remotely by the PSNI.
answer_text: The security gates from Flax Street into the Hillview Retail Park in North Belfast are the responsibility of Killultagh Estates, as the site owners. The operation of the gates are managed by security staff from the nearby Lidl store who open and close the gates on a daily basis; this has been the arrangement since the Retail Park reopened in February 2021. In October 2023, planning permission was obtained by Killultagh for the installation of new, wider, automated gates. The new gate configuration was then costed, but the scheme was deemed to be cost prohibitive. The Lidl gates are not designated as an interface security structure. Responsibility for automated gates, should they be installed at some point in the future - including the functionality for remote closure - would remain with the site owners, as custodians of the privately-owned and operated gates, not the PSNI. The safety and security of those living in and around the site is of paramount importance. My officials continue to liaise with police colleagues and the site owners to ensure that any future opening and closing arrangements for - what have become known locally as the ‘Lidl Gates’ - complement the existing security measures that are in place in the wider area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to run an Act FAST awareness campaign highlighting signs of stroke, similar to that launched by NHS England on 4 November 2024.
answer_text: Due to severe budgetary challenges, my Department has paused most health-related advertising spend in the 2023/24 and 2024/25 financial years. This decision is kept under review.  In terms of public awareness, the Public Health Agency will seek to promote FAST messaging to the public through non-paid for channels including social media platforms and media activity.The 2024/25 health budget, which was endorsed by the Assembly, has required many unpalatable and regrettable decisions to be taken. I will continue to make the case for a sustainable budget for health and social care services in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures can be taken to reduce waiting times for urgent face-to-face appointments with a gynaecology consultant.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has been asked to establish a forum to take forward the recommendations of the recent Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology. This forum has Trust, Public Health Agency (PHA) and General Practitioner (GP) representation and will oversee gynaecology service improvement and pathway development at both a local and regional level.This report details 20 regional recommendations, which this group is taking forward, along with specific recommendations for each of the Trusts.  One of these recommendations requires development and implementation of an improvement strategy to address the outpatient and surgical waiting lists, along with other related aspects including triage, validation, advice and guidance, one stop clinics.  PIFU and primary-secondary care interface.The SPPG has now written to all Trusts seeking an update against each of the recommendations, as the they seek to maximise capacity and manage demand.  They will also seek assurance of progression, through the regional forum.There is ongoing focus, by the Trusts, on addressing the waiting list, which is continuously validated to ensure that it provides an accurate picture of the number of patients who require this care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what progress is being made to secure an extension to the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to include community-based employability support programmes.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) continues to lead engagement with Whitehall on directly delivered funding streams and on successors/Post 2025 Funding. This work is endorsed by the Executive and supported by all NI Departments through the Funding Policy Inter-Departmental Working Group.I attended a meeting on 17 September in Parliament Buildings with Minister Norris from the Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government (MHCLG), Fleur Anderson (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the NIO) and Minister Archibald (DoF) where discussions around the Shared Prosperity Fund took place.  I hope, that as part of the UK Government’s planned Budget announcement on 30th October 2024 there will be some clarification on the future of SPF in Northern Ireland. I continue to engage and work collaboratively with my Executive colleagues in seeking to secure the best possible outcomes for the people in Northern Ireland to improve their employability skills and find employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support his Department provides toward the development of local community-based tourist attractions.
answer_text: Tourism NI supports all tourism businesses, including community based, to enable our industry to be innovative, sustainable, and inclusive. Tourism NI’s ongoing Industry Support programme is open to all local tourism businesses. Community groups are encouraged to make use of the free online learning provided by MyTourismNI, which offers modules on Sales & Marketing, Operational Effectiveness, Digital skills and building experiences. Community groups can also contact Tourism NI directly, for tailored support and advice on how best to take a tourism attraction project forward. Tourism NI is working with Belfast City Council on a £500k Neighbourhood Tourism Investment Programme which will run over the next 18 months and will be of particular interest to the Community Tourism sector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on securing funding from the Urban Villages Initiative for the proposed Westland Community Centre, North Belfast.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider introducing a one-way system in the neighbouring streets around Seaview Primary School, North Belfast, to ease congestion, in consultation with the school and local community.

To ask the Minister of Finance, further to the publication of the Interim Fiscal Framework on 21 May 2024, whether she has made representations to the Conservative Party and the Labour Party to secure a commitment that HM Treasury will continue to review the need for additional funding to the Northern Ireland Executive.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has (i) ownership; and (ii) maintenance responsibility for the roadway from Ligoniel Road, Belfast, leading to Ligoniel Primary School.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department does not own and therefore does not have maintenance responsibility for the roadway from Ligoniel Road, Belfast leading to Ligoniel Primary School.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the procurement exercise for the Crumlin Road Gaol Visitor and Conference Centre is (i) commencing; and (ii) concluding.
answer_text: The procurement for the new Operator for the Gaol Visitor Attraction and Conference Centre is being taken forward by Construction and Procurement Delivery CPD and commenced in February 2024 with a Pre-Market Engagement exercise. The work is ongoing, and it is expected to be complete later this year

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what measures he and his officials are taking, with other statutory agencies, to address the non-cleaning of unadopted alleyways in North Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the announcement of the closure of all other enquiry offices in Belfast police stations, for her assessment of whether Musgrave Street Police Station will have sufficient capacity to respond to in-person enquiries in a timely manner.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the Henderson Avenue Flood Alleviation Scheme in North Belfast.
answer_text: The query you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Shankill Gateway Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: My Department is working to update the Shankill Gateway project costs in order to complete a business case for the construction works.  As the Executive has now agreed a 2024/25 budget, I am working with my Senior Team to consider the implications of this budget allocation.  The funding for the Shankill Road public realm construction works will be considered within the overall DfC capital allocation and alongside existing commitments and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Clifton Gateway Public Realm Scheme in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department’s plans for the Clifton Gateway public realm improvements have been impacted by the work that DfI is taking forward on the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP2035) and the next phase of the Belfast Rapid Transit.  As Donegall Road, Clifton Street and Carlisle Circus are a significant part of the road network it is important that the scheme designs take account of and fully align with the requirements of the ETP2035.  My officials are engaging with colleagues in DfI to explore the potential of working in partnership to deliver the public realm works along Donegall Street, Clifton Street and Carlisle Circus as part of the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2, Glider route, in this area.  If this proves to be a feasible option, subject to funding, both these projects could be delivered over the next few years in a way which could minimise the disruption to everyone in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Lower Oldpark Public Realm Scheme in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has plans to deliver public realm improvements along the Lower Oldpark Road.  Since these plans were developed the Department for Infrastructure has identified Lower Oldpark Road as a route within the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan.  Officials from both Departments are working together to explore any opportunities for collaboration to deliver improved pedestrian and cycling infrastructure within the public realm project in a way that avoids nugatory works, minimises the impact on the local community, maximises the benefits gained from public investment in the area and achieves value for money.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether arrangements have been agreed for the emptying of the two rubbish bins and the cutting of the grass in Centenary Garden, Ballysillan Park, North Belfast, which was created through Urban Villages funding.
answer_text: The Centenary Garden project has established a key welcoming point into Ballysillan.  It includes new safe access to Ballysillan Primary school and has created a viewing platform providing information on the local area and markers for observed long distance views.The project has made notable improvements to the Crumlin Road junction through landscaping, natural stone paving with granite kerbs, upgrading the traffic island, decorative railings and semi-mature tree planting. Now the construction is complete, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive is responsible for the maintenance of the area. NIHE has confirmed that the grass was due to be cut by the newly appointed contractor on 2nd May 2024, and will then be cut by the contractor on a cyclical basis thereafter.  NIHE has also confirmed that bins have been emptied as per a site visit in the week commencing 29th April and will continue to be emptied regularly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of the proposed refurbishment and extension of Glenwood Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: Glenwood Primary School, Belfast, was announced under the Major Capital Works Programme in January 2013.A Planning application for the new build school was submitted in March 2022 and a decision is awaited.  The technical design and pre-tender estimate report (RIBA Stage 4) is currently being assessed by the Education Advisory Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what plans and arrangements are being considered for the gate and security gate between Flax Street and Hillview Retail Park in North Belfast; and (ii) what involvement does her Department have in this matter.
answer_text: Departmental officials are currently engaged with the owners of the Hillview Retail Park, who own the gates into the shopping complex from Flax Street. The aim of this collaboration is to have a well-designed and robust security gate system into the Hillview Retail Park from Flax Street, given the proximity of this access point to the nearby DoJ-owned interface security barrier on Flax Street (near its junction with the Crumlin Road). The owner of the gates will continue to be responsible for their operation. Planning permission for new automated gates was obtained by the owners of the Retail Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what action she has taken regarding recent anti-social behaviour by youths in the Cliftonpark Avenue and Girdwood site area, North Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is monitoring the number of in-person appointments carried out by GP practices.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients as well as decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including appointments.Under the General Practice telephone first consultation, the GP uses their clinical expertise to determine the most appropriate approach to safely addressing a patient’s needs. Where a face-to-face appointment is appropriate, this will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.   The collection of data on the number of consultations carried out weekly by GP practices commenced in October 2020.  Initially, the data collected reflected consultations by GPs and practice nurses only.  However, since October 2021, the consultation data collected has encompassed the whole GMS practice team.The collection of weekly consultation data was initially introduced in response to the Covid-19 pandemic to inform monitoring of the impact of the pandemic and support service planning.  This information has continued to be collected on a weekly basis and provides an overarching view of activity and trend information and is a proxy of overall numbers across all GP practices. Vaccination data is not separated within the data collected and will have an impact on consultation numbers over time as will other variables, including sick leave, annual leave and bank holidays. For the 4-week period ending 29 March 2024, extrapolated numbers, based on returns from an average of 84% of GP practices, show there were 808,691  GMS consultations of which 53% (426,995 ) were face-to-face.  To note that one of these weeks included a bank holiday which would decrease the number of consultations carried out over the course of the month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the commencement of spraying street weeds in North Belfast by his Department's environmental contractor; and (ii) what efforts are being made to expedite this.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department’s internal contractor began weed treatment work in selected areas of North Belfast on 13 June 2022. Problems are being experienced with our external contractor regarding the weed spraying element of their contract and meetings are to be arranged in the coming weeks to seek to address these problems.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment has been made of the larger trees along Ainsworth Avenue, Belfast, to determine (i) the need for pruning; (ii) the need to remove lower thin branches to prevent obstructions along footpaths; and (iii) the suitability of the larger trees in a narrow residential street including their replacement.
answer_text: I can confirm the trees on Ainsworth Avenue Belfast have been assessed for pruning which is expected to be carried out later this financial year.Currently epicormics (shoots from the bark area), are being removed from street trees in Belfast which will help to maximise the available footway width.My Department maintains approximately 11,500 street trees in the Greater Belfast urban area and has had previous requests to remove or relocate trees at other locations.  However, consideration regarding the removal or relocation of a street tree is normally only given instances where accessibility or safety issues have been identified.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what action his Department is taking to remove street weeds in North Belfast; and (ii) whether there is a delay in the spraying or scraping programme this year.
answer_text: My officials have issued work instructions to the Department’s environmental contractor and are awaiting confirmation of a start date for this work. Contractual discussions are ongoing with the aim of agreeing a start date by the end of June.
